skill.name: nathan-standards
skill.description: Development standards for the Nathan n8n-Jira automation system, including n8n workflow templates, Python service patterns, webhook contracts, and project conventions.
skill.summary: Apply Nathan project standards for n8n workflows, Python helpers, Jira automation, and spec-driven delivery.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are primarily Markdown code fences, n8n expression syntax, inline backticks, and documentation examples rather than executable skill code. The skill is not malicious, but it documents webhook calls, API credentials, environment secrets, and shell commands, so publication should include a warning about careful secret handling.

medium_findings.title: Webhook and Secret Handling Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents n8n webhook workflows, Jira operations, shared-secret headers, and API token environment variables. This is legitimate for the Nathan automation project, but generated implementations could expose data or credentials if users send secrets to untrusted webhook URLs or log token values.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines clearly show shared-secret authentication, API token names, and outbound webhook calls. The risk is contextual because the skill is documentation-only and does not execute these operations itself.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Matches Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Most external command detections occur in Markdown code fences, inline backtick examples, n8n expression syntax, or quick-reference commands. I found no executable script, install hook, or hidden code path that would run these commands automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is visibly inside Markdown documentation blocks or examples. The repository contains only SKILL.md and reference Markdown files, with no runnable helper script.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts point to ordinary words, JSON field names, workflow metadata, type names, and prose. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or any cryptographic API usage in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptions, JSON node structure, and Python docstring boilerplate, not cryptographic calls. No supporting semantic evidence was found for a real weak crypto issue.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: I reviewed the skill and reference files for override language, fake system messages, pre-approval claims, and instructions to skip security review. No evidence found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files contain ordinary project guidance and templates. Confidence is high because all three files were reviewed, though future versions should still be checked.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation Pattern: Outbound Webhook POST
dangerous_patterns.description: The Python reference shows an async helper that posts caller-provided parameters to a caller-provided webhook URL. This is expected for n8n automation, but users should restrict destinations and avoid sending secrets in request bodies.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code example clearly performs an outbound HTTP POST. It is not executable by the skill itself, so the concern is implementation guidance rather than active behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation Pattern: Environment Secret References
dangerous_patterns.description: The standards name webhook secrets and service API tokens. This is normal configuration documentation, but generated code should never print, store, or return those values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The locations explicitly document environment-backed secrets and token names. The risk depends on downstream use because the Markdown does not access runtime environment variables.

content.user_title: Standardize Nathan n8n and Python Workflows
content.value_statement: Nathan automation work can drift across n8n workflows, Python helpers, registries, and specs. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code consistent project standards for safer implementation.
actual_capabilities: Guides creation and review of Nathan n8n webhook workflow JSON.
Defines shared-secret validation and standard webhook response shapes.
Provides Python patterns for async webhook clients, registries, models, tests, and logging.
Documents registry.yaml command structure for workflow endpoints.
Explains agent-os spec files and task orchestration conventions.
Lists expected project layout, naming conventions, and common development commands.
limitations: It is specific to the Nathan n8n-Jira automation project.
It does not include runnable workflow generation scripts.
It does not validate live n8n, Jira, or credential configuration.
It assumes users manage secrets securely outside the skill.

use_cases.title: Build a new n8n webhook workflow
use_cases.description: Use the workflow template, secret validation node, response shape, and connection rules for a new Jira automation endpoint.

use_cases.title: Implement Python webhook helpers
use_cases.description: Apply the async HTTP client, YAML registry, Pydantic model, test, and logging patterns to Nathan helper modules.

use_cases.title: Plan a spec-driven feature
use_cases.description: Use the documented agent-os files and command sequence to shape, write, task, and orchestrate a Nathan feature.

prompt_templates.title: Review a workflow plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the nathan-standards skill to review this n8n workflow plan. Check webhook structure, secret validation, response format, and naming conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Create a webhook workflow outline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the nathan-standards skill to outline an n8n workflow for this Jira operation. Include nodes, branches, response behavior, and required registry fields.

prompt_templates.title: Design Python helper code
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the nathan-standards skill to design a Python helper for this Nathan workflow. Include async webhook behavior, typed models, registry loading, logging, and tests.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an end-to-end feature spec
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the nathan-standards skill to audit this feature spec, workflow contract, registry entry, and Python implementation plan for consistency and security gaps.

output_examples.input: Review a proposed Jira ticket lookup workflow for Nathan.
output: Flags missing shared-secret validation before the Jira node.
Recommends a success branch and a separate error response branch.
Checks that the response includes success, data, status code, and error fields.

output_examples.input: Plan a Python helper for calling Nathan n8n webhooks.
output: Suggests an async client with timeout handling and structured results.
Adds typed parameters and clear error handling for HTTP failures.
Reminds the user to pass secrets through headers and avoid logging token values.

output_examples.input: Prepare tasks for a new Nathan workflow command.
output: Breaks work into specification, registry update, workflow creation, Python integration, and tests.
Identifies required parameters and expected response behavior.
Calls out manual checks for n8n credentials and Jira permissions.

best_practices: Keep n8n as the owner of external service credentials.
Validate webhook shared secrets before any operation node runs.
Update workflow JSON, registry entries, Python helpers, and tests together.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode Jira, n8n, or LLM API tokens in generated files.
Do not bypass the standard webhook response shape for one-off workflows.
Do not mix unrelated workflow commands inside a single registry entry.

faq.question: What project is this skill for?
faq.answer: It is for the Nathan n8n-Jira automation system and its Python support code.

faq.question: Does the skill run n8n or Jira commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides standards and examples. Users run any commands in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it create complete n8n workflow JSON?
faq.answer: It can guide or draft workflow structure using the documented Nathan patterns.

faq.question: Does it include Python coding guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers async HTTP clients, registries, Pydantic models, tests, logging, and type hints.

faq.question: How should secrets be handled?
faq.answer: Secrets should stay in environment variables or managed n8n credentials. They should not be logged or committed.

faq.question: Is this a general n8n skill?
faq.answer: No. It is tailored to Nathan conventions, though some workflow patterns are reusable.
